Oklahoma Purchase Credit Card Transactions Project¶

The Office of Management and Enterprise Services in the State of Oklahoma has made its purchase credit card transactions available. This dataset contains information on purchases made through the purchase card programs administered by the state and higher education institutions.

Table of Contents¶

  • [Section 1 Data Preparation](#1)
    • 1.1 Create Data Fields
  • [Section 2 Initial observations](#2)
  • [Section 3 Feature Engineering](#3)
    • 3.1 Feature 1: The ratio to average amount
    • 3.2 Feature 2: The ratio to median amount
    • 3.3 Feature 3: Log Transformation of Transaction Amount
    • 3.4 Feature 4: Frequency of day of week
    • 3.5 Feature 5: Z-score for Transaction Amount
    • 3.6 Feature 6: Frequency of Transaction per Day
    • 3.7 Feature 7: Ratio to Merchant Category Average
    • 3.8 Feature 8: Ratio to the average amount for the agency, merchant category, month
    • 3.9 Feature 9: Combine Agency name and Cardholder last name
    • 3.10 Feature 10: Frequency of Agency Cardholder
    • 3.11 Feature 11: Vendor +amount
    • 3.12 Feature 12: Time Difference between Transaction and Posted Date
    • 3.13 Feature 13: Transaction Day of the Week
    • 3.14 Feature 14: Vendor Frequency
    • 3.15 Feature 15: One-Hot Encoding for Merchant Category

       

Section 1 Data Preparation ¶

In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
In [2]:
df = pd.read_csv('purchase_credit_card.csv')
df

In [3]:
# change column names 
df.columns = ['Year_Month', 'Agency_Number', 'Agency_Name', 'Cardholder_Last_Name',
      'Cardholder_First_Initial', 'Description', 'Amount', 'Vendor', 'Transaction_Date',
      'Posted_Date', 'Merchant_Category']

1.1 Create date fields ¶

In [4]:
#Creating separate Year and Month columns for future feature engineering
# Converting Transaction_Date and Posted_date from Python Object data type to datetime
df['Transaction_Date']=pd.to_datetime(df['Transaction_Date'])
df['Posted_Date']=pd.to_datetime(df['Posted_Date'])

df['Year_Month'].dtype
df['Year_Month']=pd.to_datetime(df['Year_Month'], format='%Y%m')
df['Year'] = df['Year_Month'].dt.year
df['Month'] = df['Year_Month'].dt.month
#Creating 'Week_Number' from 'Transaction_Date'
df['Week_Number'] = df['Transaction_Date'].dt.isocalendar().week

#Creating 'Day_of_Week' from 'Transaction_Date'
dayOfWeek={0:'Monday', 1:'Tuesday', 2:'Wednesday', 3:'Thursday', 4:'Friday', 5:'Saturday', 6:'Sunday'}
df['Day_of_Week'] = df['Transaction_Date'].dt.dayofweek.map(dayOfWeek)

df['Day_of_Week'].value_counts()
Out[4]:
Wednesday    89644
Thursday     87537
Tuesday      86919
Friday       79917
Monday       61809
Saturday     26307
Sunday       10325
Name: Day_of_Week, dtype: int64

Section 2 Initial observations ¶

In [5]:
import numpy as np
import plotly
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

# Summarize the count statistics by agency_name in df_count
df_count = df['Agency_Name'].value_counts()
df_count = pd.DataFrame(df_count).reset_index()
df_count.columns = ['Agency_Name','Count']

fig = px.bar(df_count, x='Agency_Name', y='Count', color = 'Agency_Name', width=1000, height=400)
import seaborn as sns
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
sns.barplot(y="Agency_Name", x="Count", data=df_count[1:20])
Out[5]:
<AxesSubplot:xlabel='Count', ylabel='Agency_Name'>

Section 3 Create Features ¶

3.1 Feature 1: The Ratio to the Median Amount ¶

In [6]:
# create average spending group by agency and merchant category 
stat_by_agency = df.groupby(['Agency_Name', 'Merchant_Category'])['Amount'].median() 
stat_by_agency = pd.DataFrame(stat_by_agency)
stat_by_agency.columns = ['Median_amount']
In [7]:
# add the new feature average spending to the dataset 
df1 = pd.merge(df, stat_by_agency, how='left', on=['Agency_Name', 'Merchant_Category'])
# Feature 1: ratio of amount spending and avergae spending by merchant category 
df1['agency_merchant_median_ratio_amount'] = np.where(df1['Median_amount']==0,0, df1['Amount'] / df1['Median_amount'])
df1['agency_merchant_median_ratio_amount'].describe()

df1[binned_var] = pd.qcut( df1[var],percentile)
df1['agency_merchant_median_ratio_amount']

3.3 Feature 3: Log Transformation of Transaction Amount ¶

The log transformation of transaction amount is applied to reduced the skewness. Transactions with very large or very small amounts will be more evident in the log transformated data, helping to detect the usual high or low value that could indicate as anomalies.¶
In [14]:
df['log_amount'] = np.log(df['Amount'] + 1)
df.head()

C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\arraylike.py:397: RuntimeWarning:

divide by zero encountered in log

C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\arraylike.py:397: RuntimeWarning:

invalid value encountered in log

3.4 Feature 4: Frequency of day of week ¶

This feature calculates the number of transactions made by a cardholder on specific days of the week, like Monday, Tuesday, etc. An unusual increase or decrease in the number of transactions for certain days might indicate anomalies. Most people have regular spending habits based on the day of the week, like spending on groceries every certain day in a week. Unusually high activity on certain days where the cardholder has little or no transactions might refer to suspicious behavior.¶
In [15]:
df['day_of_week'] = df['Transaction_Date'].dt.day_name()
percentiles = [0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 0.95, 0.99, 1]
df['day_of_week_frequency'] = df.groupby(['Cardholder_Last_Name', 'day_of_week'])['Amount'].transform('count')
df['day_of_week_freq_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df['day_of_week_frequency'], q=percentiles, duplicates='drop')
df[['day_of_week', 'day_of_week_frequency', 'day_of_week_freq_bucket']].head()


# Plot the distribution of time_diff_bucket
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.countplot(x='day_of_week_freq_bucket', data=df, palette='viridis')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Transaction day of week Buckets')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.5 Feature 5: Z-score for Transaction Amount ¶

Z-score helps to normalize the transaction. It is a way to see how far a particular value is from the mean value in a dataset and how many standard deviations values are away from the mean. The further a Z-score is from 0, whether positive or negative, the more extreme that the value is. For example, a value has a very high or low Z-score, it might be an outlier¶
In [16]:
df['amount_zscore'] = (df['Amount'] - df['Amount'].mean()) / df['Amount'].std()

3.6 Feature 6: Frequency of Transaction per Day ¶

The feature counts the number of transactions made by a cardholder on each day. By tracking the daily transaction frequency, it helps to identify unusual activity, like the sudden increase in transaction might be fr¶
In [17]:
#transactions per day for each cardholder
df['daily_transaction_count'] = df.groupby(['Cardholder_Last_Name', 'Cardholder_First_Initial', 'Transaction_Date'])['Amount'].transform('count')
In [18]:
df['daily_transaction_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df['daily_transaction_count'], q=percentiles, duplicates='drop')
df[['daily_transaction_count', 'daily_transaction_bucket']].head()
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.countplot(x='daily_transaction_bucket', data=df, palette='viridis')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('daily_transaction_bucket')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.7 Feature 7: Ratio to Merchant Category Average ¶

This feature calculates the ratio of each transaction amount to the average amount spent in the same merchant category. It highlights transactions that are significantly above or below the typical spending range, which can be defined as anomalies.¶
In [19]:
df1['merchant_avg_amount'] = df1.groupby('Merchant_Category')['Amount'].transform('mean')
df1['amount_to_merchant_avg'] = df1['Amount'] / df1['merchant_avg_amount']

3.8 Feature 8: Ratio to average amount for agency, merchant category, month ¶

This feature compares each transaction amount to the average spending for a given agency, merchant category, and month. The ratio helps in identifying whether the transaction amount is extremely high or low relative to the typical spending pattern.¶
In [20]:
stat_by_agency = df1.groupby(['Agency_Name', 'Merchant_Category', 'Month'])['Amount'].mean()
stat_by_agency = pd.DataFrame(stat_by_agency).reset_index()
stat_by_agency.columns = ['Agency_Name', 'Merchant_Category', 'Month', 'AVG_amount']
In [21]:
#Merge the Average to the original data
df1 = pd.merge(df1, stat_by_agency, how='left', on=['Agency_Name', 'Merchant_Category', 'Month'])
In [22]:
df1['agency_merchant_avg_ratio_amount'] = np.where(df1['AVG_amount'] == 0, 0, df1['Amount'] / df1['AVG_amount'])
Here using qcut(), ratios can be divided into distributed groups equally based on their rank. Transactions that fall into the extreme bins indicate anomalies. Transactions in the upper percentiles indicate higher expected spending, which might be a signal for a fraud transaction. The transactions in the lower percentiles indicate the unusual small or canceled transactions.¶
In [23]:
#Using pd.qcut() 
percentiles = [0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 0.95, 0.99, 1]
df1['ratio_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df1['agency_merchant_avg_ratio_amount'], q=percentiles)
df1[['Agency_Name', 'Merchant_Category', 'Month', 'Amount', 'agency_merchant_avg_ratio_amount', 'ratio_bucket']].head()
Out[23]:
Agency_Name Merchant_Category Month Amount agency_merchant_avg_ratio_amount ratio_bucket
0 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY CHARITABLE AND SOCIAL SERVICE ORGANIZATIONS 7 890.00 1.840868 (1.34, 3.417]
1 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY SHERATON 7 368.96 1.015535 (0.598, 1.34]
2 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY DIRCT MARKETING/DIRCT MARKETERS--NOT ELSEWHERE... 7 165.82 0.422770 (0.151, 0.598]
3 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY GROCERY STORES,AND SUPERMARKETS 7 96.39 1.784624 (1.34, 3.417]
4 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY STATIONERY, OFFICE SUPPLIES, PRINTING AND WRIT... 7 125.96 0.488447 (0.151, 0.598]
In [24]:
# Plotting the distribution of 'ratio_bucket' using df1
plt.figure(figsize=(5, 6))
sns.countplot(x='ratio_bucket', data=df1)  
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('ratio_bucket')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

3.9 Feature 9: Combine Agency name and Cardholder last name ¶

One of reasons for combine those two variables is different agencies may have multiple cardholders with various spending habits. Some cardholders may frequently make large purchases, but others may make small ones. By combining agency and cardholder, people can capture individual cardholder behaviors and detect the potential anomalies specific to them. For example, if a cardholder who usually spends 100 to 200 dollars suddenly has a transaction for a few thousand dollars, this might be an anomaly.¶
In [25]:
# Combine 'Agency_Name' and 'Cardholder_Last_Name' into a single feature
df['agency_cardholder'] = df['Agency_Name'] + '_' + df['Cardholder_Last_Name']
df[['Agency_Name', 'Cardholder_Last_Name', 'agency_cardholder']].head()
Out[25]:
Agency_Name Cardholder_Last_Name agency_cardholder
0 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Mason OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Mason
1 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Mason OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Mason
2 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Massey OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Massey
3 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Massey OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Massey
4 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Mauro-Herrera OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Mauro-Herrera
In [26]:
agg_stats = df.groupby('agency_cardholder')['Amount'].agg('mean').reset_index()
agg_stats.columns = ['agency_cardholder', 'mean_amount']

df = pd.merge(df, agg_stats, on='agency_cardholder', how='left')

percentiles = [0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 0.95, 0.99, 1]
df['amount_percentile'] = pd.qcut(df['Amount'], q=percentiles)
df

For qcut method, if a cardholder's tranaction amount is significantly higher or lower than most transactions like in highest 1% or lowest 1%, these transactions can be identified as anomalies.¶
In [27]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 6))
sns.countplot(x='amount_percentile', data=df)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('amount_percentile')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.title('Distribution of amount_percentile')
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.10 Feature 10: Frequency of Agency Cardholder ¶

The frequency of transactions for each agency and cardholder combination can highligh normal behavior patterns. For example, if a cardholder from a specific agency generally performs a consistent number of transactions, this count means their usual activity level. If the frequency of transaction for a particular agency_cardholder deviates significantly from the norm, it can indicate potential anomalies. For example, a cardholder who usually performs around 50 transactions monthly suddenly having 200 transactions may indicate the anomalies.¶
In [28]:
df['agency_cardholder_frequency'] = df.groupby('agency_cardholder')['agency_cardholder'].transform('count')

df[['Agency_Name', 'Cardholder_Last_Name', 'agency_cardholder', 'agency_cardholder_frequency']].head()
Out[28]:
Agency_Name Cardholder_Last_Name agency_cardholder agency_cardholder_frequency
0 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Mason OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Mason 214
1 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Mason OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Mason 214
2 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Massey OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Massey 265
3 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Massey OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Massey 265
4 OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY Mauro-Herrera OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY_Mauro-Herrera 71

3.11 Feature 11: Vendor +amount ¶

The feature indicates the relationship between vendor and amount for each month. It calculates the average transaction amount by vendor and month, and compares each individual transaction amount to this average. Extreme high or low transaction amount compared to the vendor's monthly average might indicate outliers which may be potential fraud.¶
In [29]:
stat_by_vendor = df1.groupby(['Vendor','Month'])['Amount'].mean()
stat_by_vendor = pd.DataFrame(stat_by_vendor).reset_index()
stat_by_vendor.columns = ['Vendor','Month','Vendor_meanamount']
In [30]:
#Merge the Average to the original data
df1 = pd.merge(df1, stat_by_vendor, how='left', on=['Vendor','Month'])
In [31]:
df1['vendor_month_avg_ratio_amount'] = np.where(df1['Vendor_meanamount'] == 0, 0, df1['Amount'] / df1['Vendor_meanamount'])
In [32]:
#Using pd.qcut()
percentiles = [0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 0.95, 0.99, 1]

df1['ratio_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df1['vendor_month_avg_ratio_amount'], q=percentiles)
df1[['Vendor', 'Month', 'Amount', 'vendor_month_avg_ratio_amount', 'ratio_bucket']].head()

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 6))
sns.countplot(x='ratio_bucket', data=df1)  
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('ratio_bucket')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

3.12 Feature 12: Time Difference between Transaction and Posted Date ¶

This feature indicates the time delay between the transaction date and the posted date. Longer or shorter delays might indicate anomalies. Unusual delays in posting transaction might refer to anomalies or inefficiencies in processing.¶
In [33]:
df['Transaction_Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Transaction_Date'])
df['Posted_Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Posted_Date'])

df['time_difference'] = (df['Posted_Date'] - df['Transaction_Date']).dt.days
df['time_difference']
Out[33]:
0         1
1         1
2         2
3         1
4         1
         ..
442453    3
442454    3
442455    3
442456    4
442457    3
Name: time_difference, Length: 442458, dtype: int64
In [34]:
percentiles = [0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 0.95, 0.99, 1]
df['time_diff_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df['time_difference'], q=percentiles, duplicates='drop')

df[['time_difference', 'time_diff_bucket']].head()
Out[34]:
time_difference time_diff_bucket
0 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
1 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
2 2 (1.0, 2.0]
3 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
4 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
In [35]:
# Plot the distribution of time_diff_bucket
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.countplot(x='time_diff_bucket', data=df, palette='viridis')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Time Difference Buckets')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.title('Distribution of Time Difference Buckets')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.13 Feature 13: Transaction Day of the Week ¶

This feature extracts the day of the week from the Transaction_Date. Certain agencies or cardholders have regular spending behavior based on certain day of the week, for example, more transactions on weekdays than on weekends. The large transactions on days when transactions are rarely made might indicate anomalies. For example, an agency that rarely conducts transactions on Sundays suddenly has a large volume of transactions. That might be the fraud.¶
In [36]:
df['transaction_day_of_week'] = df['Transaction_Date'].dt.dayofweek
df['day_of_week_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df['transaction_day_of_week'], q=percentiles, duplicates='drop')
df[['transaction_day_of_week', 'day_of_week_bucket']].head()
Out[36]:
transaction_day_of_week day_of_week_bucket
0 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
1 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
2 0 (-0.001, 1.0]
3 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
4 1 (-0.001, 1.0]
In [37]:
# Plot the distribution of time_diff_bucket
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.countplot(x='day_of_week_bucket', data=df, palette='viridis')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Transaction day of week Buckets')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.14 Feature 14: Vendor Frequency ¶

This feature calculates how frequently a particular vendor appears for each cardholder. Some vendors may appear regularly, while others might be used rarely. Unusual transactions with rarely used vendors might be anomalies. Using qcut() cut vendor_frequency variable into serveral percentiles, less number of count for the distribution plot might be anomalies.¶
In [38]:
#Frequency of each vendor for each cardholder
df['vendor_frequency'] = df.groupby(['Cardholder_Last_Name', 'Vendor'])['Vendor'].transform('count')
df['vendor_frequency_bucket'] = pd.qcut(df['vendor_frequency'], q=percentiles, duplicates='drop')
df[['vendor_frequency', 'vendor_frequency_bucket']].head()
Out[38]:
vendor_frequency vendor_frequency_bucket
0 2 (0.999, 6.0]
1 4 (0.999, 6.0]
2 2 (0.999, 6.0]
3 8 (6.0, 32.0]
4 4 (0.999, 6.0]
In [39]:
# Plot the distribution of time_diff_bucket
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.countplot(x='vendor_frequency_bucket', data=df, palette='viridis')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Transaction day of week Buckets')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.15 Feature 15: One-Hot Encoding for Merchant Category ¶

One-Hot encoding for merchant category helps making text into number, which helps following modeling process¶
In [40]:
# One-hot encode 'Merchant_Category'
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['Merchant_Category'], prefix='MCC', drop_first=True)

Merge existing columns including one-hot Encoding!¶

In [41]:
# Get the column names for each dataframe
df1_columns = df1.columns
df2_columns = df2.columns
df_columns = df.columns
common_columns_12 = df1_columns.intersection(df2_columns)
common_columns_123 = common_columns_12.intersection(df_columns)
merged_df = pd.merge(df1, df2, how='outer', on=list(common_columns_12))
merged_df = pd.merge(merged_df, df, how='outer', on=list(common_columns_123))
In [42]:
merged_df
